When GIS works in extremely cold regions, a huge temperature gradient is generated along the insulator, causing further surface charge accumulation, but its investigations are scarce due to the tough measurement conditions. Herein, a surface charging simulation model involves electro-thermal-fluid multiphysics fields and surface charging differential equations is established to analyze the surface charging in a cold environment, and a conventional high conductivity coating and a nonlinear conductivity coating design are applied to the insulator to suppress charge accumulation. The numerical simulation indicates a large temperature gradient is produced along the insulator by decreasing environment temperature, increasing current, or increasing heat transfer coefficients, causing lots of charges to accumulate near the ground electrode. The application of nonlinear conductivity coating effectively suppress charge accumulation in extremely cold environments, and the maximum charge density drops by 41.5% and 50.4% for convex and concave insulators compared to uncoated insulator, which is superior to conventional high conductivity coating that exhibits 31.6% and 37.7%. The charging path analysis indicates although conventional high conductivity coating reduces charges near the ground electrode, it elevates charge density at other positions. For nonlinear conductivity coating, it only regulates charges near the electrode with the large electric field. This work presents charge accumulation characteristics and its inhibition strategy for insulators in extremely cold environments, which are beneficial for protecting GIS secure operation in extreme conditions.

title: Collaborative Truck-Drone Routing with Multi-visit via Surrogate-Assisted Bi-level Optimization_supp1-3662657.pdf
authors: Tong Guo
abstract: The development of drone techniques has extensive applications to fields like last-mile parcel delivery. For economic and efficiency advantages, collaborative truck-drone systems have attracted great attention and formulated as truck-drone routing problems. In this paper, a multi-visit traveling salesman problem with drones is investigated. We decompose it into a two-layer structure and formulate it as a new bi-level model (Bi-MTSPD). The major advantage of the bi-level model is its ability to represent the complex interactions between truck and drone routes by dispatching the drones under the premise of truck routes. To solvethe bi-level model, we propose a novel surrogate-assisted bi-level optimization method. The upper-level subproblem can be treated as a traveling salesman problem with customer assignment, and high-quality truck routes are identified by the K-nearest neighbor-based surrogate model with newly proposed features in order to allocate more computational resources to promising upper-level solutions. For the lower-level drone location routing problem (DLR), a customized memetic algorithm is developed to optimize the drone routes for the corresponding truck route. Comprehensive experimental results show that the proposed algorithm significantly outperforms the existing state-of-the-art algorithms across most benchmark instances. Further analysis verifies the congruence of the bi-level optimization for MTSP-D and the effectiveness of upper-level and lower-level solvers.

title: Learning from Heterophilic Graphs: A Spectral Theory Perspective on the Impact of Self-Loops and Parallel Edges
authors: Kushal Bose, Swagatam Das
abstract: Graph heterophily poses a formidable challenge to the performance of Message-passing Graph Neural Networks (MP-GNNs). The familiar low-pass filters like Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) face performance degradation, which can be attributed to the blending of the messages from dissimilar neighboring nodes. The performance of the low-pass filters on heterophilic graphs still requires an in-depth analysis. In this context, we update the heterophilic graphs by adding a number of self-loops and parallel edges. We observe that the eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian decrease and increase, respectively, by increasing the number of self-loops and parallel edges. We conduct several studies regarding the performance of GCN on various benchmark heterophilic networks by adding either self-loops or parallel edges. The studies reveal that the GCN exhibited either increasing or decreasing performance trends on adding self-loops and parallel edges. In light of the studies, we established connections between the graph spectra and the performance trends of the low-pass filters on the heterophilic graphs. The graph spectra characterize the essential intrinsic properties of the input graph, like the presence of connected components, sparsity, average degree, cluster structures, etc. Our work is adept at seamlessly evaluating graph spectrum and properties by observing the performance trends of the low-pass filters without pursuing the costly eigenvalue decomposition. The theoretical foundations are also discussed to validate the impact of adding self-loops and parallel edges on the graph spectrum.

title: From the Society: President's Message
authors: Shervin Shirmohammadi
abstract: Dear members, colleagues, and friends of the IEEE Instrumentation and Measurement Society (IMS), Welcome to another year with IMS and all its exciting activities! This is my second year as the IMS President. You may recall that exactly one year ago in this column I set out some objectives for my term as the President to accelerate IMS's growth and improve its quality even further. I am pleased to report that much has been achieved since, which I will summarize here.

title: A Zero-Shot Learning Framework for Accurate Appendix Segmentation Based on SAM (AAZ-SAM)_supp1-3663199.docx
authors: Bingsheng Huang
abstract: The deep learning-based automatic segmentation of the appendix CT images faces the challenge of scarce annotations, necessitating the development of high-precision segmentation methods with minimal or no reliance on annotated data. To address this, we propose a zero-shot learning framework for appendix segmentation based on the segmentation large model Segment Anything Model (SAM), named AAZ-SAM, which consists of two modules: 1) the Guidance Information Expansion Module (GIEM) generates complete layer prompt information through linear interpolation of three key layer prompt boxes (first, middle, and last layers), eliminating the need for layer-by-layer annotated prompt boxes, thereby improving clinical efficiency. 2) the Feature Focusing Module (FFM) utilizes Gaussian focusing to concentrate the model’s attention, enhancing segmentation performance without the need for model fine-tuning, achieving an improvement in the DSC from 0.817 to 0.836 on the internal test sets, and 0.634 to 0.668 on the external test sets. This study demonstrates that the innovative strategies can improve the performance of large models in medical imaging, enhancing the efficiency and segmentation accuracy of SAM in appendix segmentation tasks.

title: UTracker: Learning Visuomotor Policies for Underwater Active Target Tracking Via Imitation Learning and Diffusion Model
authors: Ao Meng, Lin Hong, Yunxuan Feng, Zijie Ling, Xin Li, Liang Hu
abstract: Active visual tracking of underwater non-cooperative targets is a challenging task for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) due to the complexity of underwater environments and the unpredictable dynamics of target motion. To address this challenge, this paper proposes UTracker, a novel framework for learning visuomotor policies tailored to underwater active target tracking. UTracker employs a two-stage training pipeline to enhance data efficiency and generalization. First, a state-based expert policy is trained in simulated underwater environments to perform underwater target tracking, and then used to generate paired image–action data as expert demonstrations. Second, a visuomotor policy is distilled from expert demonstrations via imitation learning, with diffusion models incorporated to refine action sequences and improve robustness against observation noise and dynamic perturbations. Extensive simulation studies show that UTracker outperforms baseline methods in challenging underwater scenarios. Moreover, sim-to-real experiments on a real AUV validate that the learned visuomotor policy reliably tracks non-cooperative targets in real-world environments. Video and code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Ice-mao/RL_AUV_tracking.git</uri>.

title: Kinematic Coordination and Muscle Synergy Patterns of Grasping during Lower-limb Locomotion_supp4-3663964.mp4
authors: Yan Huang

title: Brain Monitoring Devices for Neuroscience: A Review of Current Technologies
authors: Claudio Salvalaio, Eduardo Maciel, Felipe Bitencourt, Eduarda Tessari, Isadora Nascimento, Daniela Pohren, Aline Pagnussat, Isabel Siqueira, Alcyr Oliveira
abstract: This scoping review presents a device-level characterization of 98 commercially available wireless brain monitoring systems, focusing on electroencephalography (EEG) and functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) applications in neuroscience and neurorehabilitation. A structured literature search was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines and registered in the Open Science Framework. Searches were performed across five databases (IEEE Xplore, Scopus, PubMed/MEDLINE,Web of Science, and Embase) between March and August 2025. From 5,069 retrieved records, 3,748 remained after duplicate removal. After consolidation of studies reporting multiple devices, 3,164 unique articles were screened, of which 2,100 reported the use of identifiable brain monitoring devices, corresponding to an inclusion rate of 66.37%. Based on these studies and complementary manufacturer documentation, a dataset comprising 98 commercially available devices was compiled and analyzed in terms of acquisition-level characteristics, research adoption, and application-oriented readiness. Research adoption was quantified using DOI-indexed publication counts, revealing a highly skewed distribution in which the five most frequently used devices accounted for 59.10% of all studies. Regression analysis controlling for device age showed that open access to raw data and software interfaces was the only factor significantly associated with higher research adoption. A TOPSIS-based multi-criteria decision analysis was applied to compare devices according to acquisition-level suitability. The results indicate a shift toward portable and software-accessible neurotechnology, alongside persistent differences between consumer- and research-grade systems in data access, synchronization, and real-time capability. This review provides a reproducible framework to support informed device selection in neuroscience and neurorehabilitation.

title: Fine-grained Detection for Bitcoin Mixing Services using Heterogeneous Representation Learning
authors: Yan Wu, Jiahang Sun, Zhen Li, Cong Wu, Zhe Guo, Yi Ding, Jun Jin, Jincheng An, Chuan Zhang, Zijian Zhang, Meng Li, Liehuang Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: With the widespread adoption of cryptocurrencies, exemplified by Bitcoin, mixing services have been increasingly misused for illicit activities such as fraud, extortion, and money laundering. These services cut off the direct link between input and output addresses to conceal publicly available transaction traces on the blockchain, thereby evading regulatory investigations. In recent years, significant efforts have been made to deanonymize Bitcoin mixing services (BMS). While these efforts have yielded some achievements in service detection, they still face several challenges, such as vague theoretical definitions, coarse-grained identification capabilities, and insufficient labeling information. To address these challenges, this paper formalizes the notion of BMS with a syntax, security model, and goals, providing a universal definition for evaluating service anonymity and detection feasibility. We also propose a fine-grained detection framework for BMS, called BMS-FDET, which leverages heterogeneous Bitcoin transactional networks and multiple attention mechanisms to identify mixing transactions across different services. To improve the model's detection capabilities and compare performance, a sufficient ground-truth dataset with over 596,200 entries covering more than 15 mixing services is built from extensive data sources. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of BMS-FDET over existing methods.

title: Linking Physical Fidelity to Downstream Performance in Physics-Informed Fault Diagnosis
authors: Enzo Nicolás Spotorno, Josafat Leal Filho, Antônio Augusto Fröhlich
abstract: A critical question in Scientific Machine Learning is whether improving a Physics-Informed Neural Network’s (PINN) physical fidelity translates into downstream task performance. We investigate this link using a three-stage diagnostic framework: PINN-based residual generation, Extreme Value Theory anomaly detection, and Siamese Neural Network (SNN) open-set recognition. To overcome optimization costs, we propose a hierarchical three-phase evaluation protocol that progressively filters models based on physical fidelity (Phase 1), linear separability (Phase 2), and downstream robustness (Phase 3). Applying this to a ring bearing system, we evaluated adaptive PINNs against rigorous, architecturally identical data-driven baselines. Results revealed a paradox: unconstrained baselines achieved superior linear separability on known faults yet over-generalized on novel ones. In contrast, PINNs selected for high physical fidelity produced residuals with lower initial separability but a significantly richer latent structure and topological compactness. We demonstrate that the physics loss acts as an inductive bias for invariance, preventing "shortcut" features. This results in enhanced learnability, quantified by the improvement in cluster quality, leading to a state-of-the-art OSR F1-score of 84.0%. This work empirically establishes that optimizing for physical fidelity improves system robustness and provides a tractable methodology for guiding PINN-based applications.

title: Explainable Molecular Property Prediction: Aligning Chemical Concepts with Predictions via Language Models
authors: Zhenzhong Wang, Zehui Lin, Wanyu Lin, Ming Yang, Minggang Zeng, Kay Chen Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Providing explainable molecular property predictions is critical for many scientific domains, such as drug discovery and material science. Though transformer-based language models have shown great potential in accurate molecular property prediction, they neither provide chemically meaningful explanations nor faithfully reveal the molecular structure-property relationships. In this work, we develop a framework for explainable molecular property prediction based on language models, dubbed as Lamole, which can provide chemical concepts-aligned explanations. We take a string-based molecular representation — Group SELFIES — as input tokens to pre-train and fine-tune our Lamole, as it provides chemically meaningful semantics. By disentangling the information flows of Lamole, we propose considering both self-attention weights and gradients for better quantification of each chemically meaningful substructure's impact on the model's output. To make the explanations more faithful to the structure-property relationship, we then carefully craft a marginal loss to explicitly optimize the explanations to align with the chemists' annotations. We bridge the manifold hypothesis with the elaborated marginal loss to prove that the loss can align the explanations with the tangent space of the data manifold, leading to concept-aligned explanations. Experimental results over eight datasets demonstrate Lamole can achieve comparable prediction accuracy and boost the explanation accuracy by up to $14.3\%$, being the state-of-the-art in explainable molecular property prediction. To further illustrate the actionable utility of the explanations derived from Lamole, we integrated the framework with an evolutionary algorithm. This integration established an interpretable optimization pipeline for molecular editing, demonstrating that Lamole functions beyond simple post-hoc analysis but serves as a practical guide for molecule discovery. The code is available at the provided link: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Lamole-7482

title: Geomagnetic Vector Navigation Based on Interactive Marginalized Particle Filter
authors: Xu Liu, Dixiang Chen, Yujing Xu, Qingfa Du, Bo Huang, Yujie Xiang, Qi Zhang, Zhongyan Liu, Mengchun Pan, Jiafei Hu, Zhuo Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Aeromagnetic vector navigation has the potential to significantly improve the autonomous navigation of the aircraft, especially for low-altitude aircraft, in the environment of restricted or denied satellite positioning. By fusing geomagnetic vector measurements with inertial navigation data, it enables joint estimation and correction of INS error states. The key of high-precision geomagnetic vector navigation is to realize the cooperative correction of position and attitude errors of navigation system through geomagnetic vector navigation filter, which significantly reduces the influence of vehicle space position and attitude errors on geomagnetic vector measurement, and then obtains high-precision position and attitude estimation. However, the nonlinear and non-Gaussian characteristics are superimposed with large initial position error and magnetic measurement error, which makes it difficult to maintain high accuracy and stability of geomagnetic vector navigation filtering. To solve the above problems, a geomagnetic vector navigation strategy based on interactive marginalized particle filtering (IMPF) is proposed. The two-component geomagnetic matching that is insensitive to heading errors is combined with the marginalized particle filter (MPF) framework. The MPF framework enables accurate estimation of the navigation error-state vector under nonlinear and non-Gaussian conditions. In addition, considering the comprehensive magnetic measurement error term and the two-component geomagnetic matching position constraint, the filter parameters are adaptively modified, thus improving the robustness of the geomagnetic vector navigation filter to the initial position error of the vehicle and the magnetic measurement error. The above geomagnetic vector navigation strategy can realize the cooperative optimal estimation of vehicle position and attitude under large initial position error and magnetic measurement error. The semi-measured driving simulation experiment based on the measured geomagnetic vector maps shows that this method can achieve higher accuracy and stability of geomagnetic navigation.

title: Estimating daily wet Nitrate deposition across China considering under-cloud and in-cloud scavenging effects from satellite observations_supp1-3663637.docx
authors: Zhang Xiuying
abstract: Satellite remote sensing offers a promising approach to estimate atmospheric nitrate (NO₃⁻) wet deposition with high spatiotemporal resolution. Considering the under-cloud and in-cloud scavenging effects on NO₃⁻ by the rainfall, this study developed a machine learning model to estimate daily wet NO₃⁻ depositions across China based on atmospheric NO2 columns from the Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) observations, NO₃⁻ concentrations in rainfall, and meteorological data. Results demonstrated that the proposed model achieved high accuracy, with cross-validation of R = 0.78 (p < 0.01) and RMSE = 0.09 kg N ha⁻¹ for single-precipitation event and R = 0.90 (p < 0.01) and RMSE = 0.26 kg N ha⁻¹ for monthly NO₃⁻ depositions; rainfall amount, under-cloud nitrate concentrations, and socioeconomic factors collectively dominated the spatial and temporal variations of NO₃⁻ depositions, while in-cloud nitrate concentration exhibited great contributions in less-N polluted areas such as northwestern China; deposition fluxes exhibit an increasing gradient from the northwest to southeastern China, ranging from 0.13 to 81.76 kg N ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹ and concentrated from June to September. This work pioneers the contributions from in-cloud and under-cloud scavenging processes on wet NO₃⁻ depositions, providing a scalable tool for regional nitrogen deposition monitoring and environmental management.

title: CarambolaCam: A Hybrid Graph-Based Deep Learning Framework Using GCN and GAT for Carambola Leaf and Fruit Disease Classification
authors: Rejowan Arifin Nayeem, S. M. Abdullah Al Muhib, Sabbir Hossain Durjoy, Prottoy Kundu, Arnob Das Shacha, Md. Emon Shikder, Muha. Humayet Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Starfruit (Carambola) is a tropical fruit, which is at high risk of various foliar and fruit diseases that affect yield and quality. In order to deal with this problem, we present an accurate and efficient hybrid deep learning model combining Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and Graph Attention Networks (GAT) for disease detection. Our GCN-GAT model combines both spatial characteristics as well as the relational dependencies between the image data, which leads to an enhanced classification performance. It showed high test accuracies of 96.44% on the original dataset and 97.42% on the augmented dataset. We further compared our method with popular CNN architectures such as InceptionV3, DenseNet121, MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, VGG16 and VGG19 showing that our hybrid approach surpasses these baseline methods. We implemented a mobile application (CarambolaCam) for potential real-world usage, allowing the farmers to detect diseases based on images of leaves and fruits. These findings further identify the power of graph-based learning in agricultural disease diagnostics and its applicability towards building low-cost, field-ready smart farming solutions.

title: A Novel Abstraction Framework for Efficient Radio Resource Management Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Manoj Kumar N, Manjunath B Talawar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: This paper gives a complete framework that combines deep reinforcement learning algorithms [1] with advanced methods for modeling abstract state spaces to solve these problems in the best way possible. We use a Markov Decision Process to define the resource allocation problem and come up with new ways to group things, such as uniform, similarity-based, and dissimilarity-based strategies. The proposed method utilizes Deep Q-Networks and Actor-Critic techniques [2] to enhance resource allocation decisions while adhering to quality of service standards. Extensive simulations reveal that our abstract model performs nearly optimally with greatly diminished computational complexity, making it appropriate for real-time implementation in dynamic wireless settings.

title: Optimizer-Based Performance Evaluation of Deep Learning Models for Loan Eligibility Prediction
authors: Banala Saritha, G Purnachandrarao, Yadam Srujan Kumar, Vaddi Venkata Dinesh, Thummagunta Vasantha Rani, Yaramanedi Jayanth Kumar
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Information Technology and Communication Networks (GITCON)
abstract: Reliable for loan eligibility prediction is necessary for banks in order to optimize the way they lend and reduce the risk of loan default. Traditional verification techniques are not keeping up with the increasing number of applications and customer demand for quick, reliable evaluations. Deep Neural Network (DNNs), Convolutional Neural Network (CNNs), and DenseNet are the three deep learning architectures that are studied and compared in our study. To classify loan eligibility outcomes, by training models using various Optimizers, such as Adam, Stochastic Gradient Descent, RMSprop, and Adagrad.To provide an adequate evaluation, we evaluate the models using a variety of performance metrices, which include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. Our goal is to highlight that CNN models with Adam regularly outperform different strategies, obtained better generalization and predictive accuracy. On other hand, DenseNet models performed well on every metric. These results highlight how deep learning approaches can be used to automate loan eligibility predictions, providing a scalable method for improving loan risk evaluations and increasing operational effectiveness in the financial services industry.

title: LSTM and CNN based Environmental Assessment Model for Analyzing Rainwater Harvesting in Urban Area
authors: S. Gautham, P. William Robert, S.Chandrasekaran
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: Rainwater harvesting represents an essential water conservation approach because it addresses the water-related problems faced by urban which cope with scarce resources and irregular rain patterns. RainImpact-AI functions as a computer-generated method for evaluating rainwater harvesting systems through hydrological simulations accompanied by machine learning and deep learning models. The model uses LSTM for rainfall prediction with 97.45% accuracy and CNN for rooftop rainwater structure identification reaching 98.34% accuracy and Random Forest models achieve 96.78% accuracy in groundwater recharge assessment. AI-enhanced SWAT and HEC-HMS tools combined in hybrid hydrological modeling produce reliable urban water flow and infiltration simulations. The RainImpact-AI system shows notable enhancements in groundwater recharge as it increases water levels by 42.5% across ten different urban zones. The AI-driven simulations for rainwater collection yield accurate results when compared to measured harvest volumes because they deviate only by 1.57% to 2.02% from the actual quantities. Policy evaluation through the study shows that AI-supported rainwater management approaches can reach 97.5% optimal efficiency in saving urban water supply under ideal implementation conditions. The combination of environmental modeling with AI at RainImpact-AI system generates data-based solutions to maximize RWH policies while supporting sustainability and stopping urban water scarcity. The model establishes itself as an adaptable decision-making tool which supports urban planning authorities and policy officials by delivering immediate predictive analysis solutions for better resource water management practices.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Prediction from Retinal Images Using ResNet18 CNN with Proposed Detection Extension
authors: Kudumula Bhavyasree Reddy, Padamati Sricharan Reddy, Gajagowni Varshith Goud, Mohd Sallahuddin, Saroja Kumar Rout, Kottu Santosh Kumar
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Information Technology and Communication Networks (GITCON)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) remain the primary cause of death worldwide, needing precise and early risk predic- tion approaches in order to minimize mortality and improve patient outcomes. In this article, we present a deep learning- based framework to classify cardiovascular risk levels into Low, Medium, and High categories using retinal fundus pictures. A pretrained ResNet18 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that has been optimized for three-class classification utilizing a curated and enhanced retinal scan dataset serves as the foundation for the model. With a 92.5 percent overall accuracy, great precision, and F1-scores in every class, the CNN showed excellent generalization. This non-invasive technique provides a trustworthy substitute for conventional diagnostic techniques by taking use of the retina’s vascular and structural characteristics, which are recognized to represent systemic cardiovascular disorders.Furthermore, we suggest including YOLOv8-based object identification in subsequent research with the goal of improving model explainability by identifying crucial areas such optic disc abnormalities or constricted arteries. The model offers a scalable, interpretable, and clinically relevant solution for early cardiovascular risk assessment, and the results show that it has the potential to be implemented in real-time in screening programs and preventive healthcare.

title: Tea Leaves Under the Lens of AI: A Comparative Deep Learning Perspective
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Tea agriculture is a very crucial part of the agricultural economy to which proper identification of leaf diseases is relevant when confirming the quality and sustainability of yield. This paper will discuss how good the classifiers can be on examining specific leaf diseases of tea using images, using deep learning models. The four state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN) models, which consist of DenseNet201, ResNet152V2, VGG19, and Xception, were used in comparing them. DenseNet201 obtained the best performance with an accuracy of (94.03%) surpassing all other networks namely ResNet152V2 (80.12%), Xception (72%), and VGG19 (80.43%) in novel network architecture task. The findings prove that deep CNN architectures can be an effective tool in enabling smart farming and precision plant health diagnosis. The research can offer practical information towards using a model when deciding where to implement AI-enabled plant disease, especially in the tea sector.

title: Full-Stack Architecture for Globally Distributed Systems: Leveraging Multi-Regional Cloud Infrastructure
authors: Mohit Menghnani
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Information Technology and Communication Networks (GITCON)
abstract: As global access to web applications increases, ensuring low latency, high availability, and scalability across regions in cloud infrastructure has become essential. In the current digital environment, creating scalable and resilient full stack apps is essential to provide dependable, user-friendly, and high-performance experiences. With the growing demand for low-latency and highly available web services across global user bases, this study explores the design and deployment of a multi- regional cloud-based full-stack application architecture. The system leverages React.js for the frontend (deployed on Netlify’s CDN), Node.js with Express.js for region-specific backend APIs (hosted on Render in Frankfurt and Singapore), and MongoDB Atlas configured as a globally distributed database. The methodology involves implementing dynamic geo-routing, real- time API logging, and CI/CD pipelines for seamless deployment and failover handling. Results indicate a significant reduction in global latency (from 310 ms to 180 ms), near-zero downtime during updates, and improved responsiveness as measured by frontend and end-to-end stack performance metrics such as TTFB, FCP, and API latency. The study concludes that multi- regional deployment not only enhances user experience through faster content delivery and improved reliability but also provides scalable and observable infrastructure for modern cloud-native applications. The key contribution lies in demonstrating a modular, globally distributed architecture with quantifiable performance gains, serving as a practical reference model for future resilient and scalable application development.

title: Predicting Financial Market Movements in Stock Using AI-Driven Time Series Forecasting Techniques
authors: Harshit Kohli, Kapil Kumar Goyal, Ruhul Quddus Majumder, Rajesh Sura
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Information Technology and Communication Networks (GITCON)
abstract: Investors have a hard time making educated selections when using traditional forecasting models since these models can't handle the intricacies of the financial markets. The stock market's non-linear and erratic behavior has long made accurate prediction difficult. Time series forecasting approaches powered by artificial intelligence have become very effective tools for anticipating stock price changes as machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) have progressed. This paper suggests an enhanced predictive framework of Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) to enhance accuracy in stock price forecasts of Apple Inc. The data is gathered on Yahoo financial site and covers more than 40 years with essential stock variables. XGBoost model was compared to other conventional models based on various performance metrics. XGBoost showed the best accuracy score of 95.30 per cent with R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) = 0.1385, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) = 0.5570, Mean Squared Error (MSE) = 0.3767, and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) = 0.4040. In comparison, the Polynomial Regression and the LSTM performed poorly with R2 of 76.65 percent and 76.09 percent respectively and larger error. The findings indicate that XGBoost is the superior model to rely on accuracy and foreseeing power when anticipating stock prices. This work throws some light on the role of ML in financial forecasting, which is a tremendous assistance to analysts and investors to make data-driven decisions.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Image-Based Sentiment Analysis for Enhanced Emotion Detection on Social Media
authors: B Venkateswarlu, Katla Swetha, Bolleddu Sudhakar, Renuka Parnandhi, B Prasanthi, Bidyutlata Sahoo
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: Sentiment analysis is essential in social media analysis to understand user perspectives on various issues. Social media sentiment analysis through text, speech, and photos is increasingly essential in many applications. As with words, social media users submit more photos and videos. Many sentiment analysis (SA) studies employ textual data. Images convey emotions better than text. A SA model based on social-media photographs is desired. We used VGG19, ResNet50V2, and DenseNet-121 transfer learning (TL) models for image SA. They were fine-tuned by freezing and unfreezing layers and improved by regularization. The Crowdflower collection comprises URLs of Twitter-Based photos with sentiment polarity. We also compare these pre-trained algorithms' picture sentiment prediction on our dataset. Our fine-tuned TL models for VGG19, ResNet50V2, and DenseNet-121 have 0.83, 0.85, and 0.92 accuracy. Our approach outperformed earlier visual sentiment analysis methods using machine and deep learning by 8% to 15%. The DenseNet-121 model predicted picture sentiment better than VGG19 and ResNet50V2.

title: CNN-Transformer Architecture with XAI for Vision-Based UAV Detection in Security Applications
authors: Md. Abdur Rouf, Sohel Rana
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are becoming increasingly popular in civilian, commercial, and military domains, intensifying the need for accurate, real-time drone detection to protect sensitive airspaces. This study presents a hybrid deep learning architecture that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with a Transformer backbone for enhanced UAV image detection. The approach leverages CNNs for efficient local feature extraction and Transformers architecture for capturing global contextual dependencies, improving both accuracy and robustness. Experiments were conducted on a publicly available UAV dataset of over 12,000 samples prepared in both COCO JSON and image–label formats for broad model compatibility. We compared with multiple architectures, including YOLOv11n (93.9%), YOLOv8 (93.7%), Faster R-CNN (76.42%), RetinaNet (79.7%), Faster R-CNN MobileNetV3 (79.43%), ViT (90.32%), and a standalone CNN (95%). The proposed CNN + Transformer model achieved the highest accuracy of 97%, outperforming all baselines. These results prove the effectiveness of combining local and global feature modeling for UAV detection. The proposed method offers a promising vision-based solution for real-time monitoring in defense, law enforcement, and critical infrastructure security.

title: Automated Plastic Waste Detection Using Advanced Deep Learning Frameworks
authors: Gulam Jakaria, Md. Ferdous, Tahsin Ahmed Chowdhury, Hirak Mondal, Mrinal Kanti Baowaly
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: The increasing accumulation of plastic waste poses one of the most significant threats to both human health and the environment. In recent years, microplastics have been detected ubiquitously—in food, water, and even the air—emphasizing the pressing need for efficient control and monitoring methods. To address this challenge, this study presents a deep learning–based system for automated plastic waste detection from images, with the objective of improving accuracy and efficiency in identifying plastic materials. For experimentation, we used WPRD dataset (combined of WaDaBa and the Plastic Recyclable Detection dataset) and employed the YOLOv11 (You Only Look Once) object detection framework. Five major categories of plastic waste were considered: Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (PEHD), Polypropylene (PP), Polystyrene (PS), and Ultra-High Temperature (UHT) packaging. The system demonstrates strong performance, achieving high precision and recall in classifying plastic objects within diverse environmental contexts. Specifically, the YOLOv11m architecture attained a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 94.8%, underscoring the effectiveness of the proposed approach for automated plastic waste detection.

title: Performance Analysis and Comparative Study of Traditional, Ensemble, and Hybrid Machine Learning Models for Sustainable Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Classification
authors: Deepali Saini, Sumit Kushwaha
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) continues to be one of the leading causes of illness worldwide, where early and reliable classification plays a crucial role in improving patient care. This study applies machine learning to address this challenge by comparing traditional classifiers such as Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, XGBoost, and K-Nearest Neighbor with ensemble methods like Bagging, Voting, and Stacking, along with a newly designed hybrid model. To ensure stronger performance, hyperparameters of all base classifiers were tuned using the randomized search technique and further used in ensemble and hybrid model design. The results indicate that ensemble methods added significant strength by leveraging multiple learners, and the proposed hybrid classifier emerged as the best performer, achieving a leading accuracy of 99.5%, outperforming other approaches. The model performance was evaluated and compared using accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score values. Beyond performance comparison, this study emphasizes sustainability, highlighting how efficient and scalable prediction frameworks can reduce computational load and ease the financial burden on patients, supporting long-term healthcare solutions. This work supports the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals—SDG 3.0 and SDG 9.0, focusing on Good Health and Well-being and Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure, respectively—promoting accessible, cost-effective, and sustainable approaches to disease diagnosis.

title: Stress Prediction Among University Students Using Swarm Intelligence and Explainable AI
authors: Abdullah Raian, Shahriar Siddique Ayon, Aditya Rajbongshi, B. M. Mredul Arafin, Farhana Islam, Rubel Sheikh
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: University students are increasingly grappling with stress, a multifaceted issue that can significantly impact their academic performance and mental health. Prior research on student stress has been hindered by inadequate feature measures and a lack of rigorous feature-selection, optimization, and explainability methods. To address these gaps, we propose a transparent, high-precision framework for predicting stress levels among 2,028 Bangladeshi university students by integrating advanced feature-selection, Swarm Intelligence optimization, and Explainable AI (XAI). We first employ Principal Component Analysis and Information Gain (PCA-IG) to extract the most informative items from the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10), then optimize a logistic-regression classifier’s hyperparameters using the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm. Against conventional baselines, our ABC-tuned model achieves 98.99% accuracy and a 99.01% F1-score on hold-out data, underscoring its exceptional predictive performance. Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) based interpretations further reveal that stress frequency, coping difficulty, and perceived loss of control are the primary drivers of model decisions, providing actionable insights for targeted interventions. Overall, this approach offers a robust, interpretable tool for early stress detection and personalized mental-health support, equipping educators and policymakers with data-driven strategies to foster student well-being.

title: Jailbreaking Large Language Models: A Red Teaming Perspective
authors: Md. Robiul Islam Niloy, SM Mehady Hasan, Youhei Akimoto, A. S. M. Sabiqul Hassan, Md. Wardun Islam Noon
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Despite their remarkable progress in natural language processing, Large Language Models (LLMs) are nevertheless susceptible to jailbreaking—adversarial prompts that circumvent security measures to elicit inappropriate or malicious content. We introduce a taxonomy that covers prompt-based, optimisation-based, multi-turn, and LLM-to-LLM assaults in this paper’s thorough red teaming research of jailbreaks. Our tests employ statistical analysis and rigorous multi-run conditions to evaluate four sample LLMs, encompassing both proprietary and open-source systems. According to the results, the most successful suffix attacks are optimisation-based, achieving up to 92.4% success on LLaMA-2 and 65.1% on ChatGPT-4. Attack success rates more than double over eight turns in multi-turn exploits, indicating that alignment deteriorates with discourse length. We also examine defence measures, demonstrating that anomaly detection, albeit at the expense of an 8.2% false positive rate on benign inputs, reduces attack success rates by 44% on average. These results show that current protections are still lacking and emphasise the trade-offs between robustness and usability. We suggest forward-looking approaches, such as federated red teaming, interpretability-driven diagnostics, uniform benchmarks, and adaptive defences, to solve these issues. Through the integration of governance insights and empirical evaluation, our work helps create LLMs that are more reliable and secure.

title: MB-CNN-LSTM-ATTN: An Optimized Deep Learning Model for Sleep Disorder Classification
authors: Md. Samiul Islam, Md Mehraz Hosen, Md Bahar Uddin, Md. Hossain, Md. Maniruzzaman, Alifya Akhtar
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Sleep disorders, including obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and insomnia, have serious implications for cognitive health and quality of life. Conventional diagnostic techniques, such as polysomnography, are expensive and are not available to the majority of the population. Early detection of these disorders is essential for mitigating long-term health consequences, yet traditional diagnostic methods remain out of reach for many. In this paper, we present MB-CNN-LSTM-ATTN, a novel deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short Term Memory (LSTM), and attention mechanism to reliably diagnose sleep disorders. The model is employed to analyze the Sleep Health and Lifestyle Dataset, and the class imbalance problem is addressed using SMOTEENN, while feature selection is performed using Gradient Boosting. A classification accuracy of 97.65% was obtained by the model, and it exhibited better precision (96.87%), recall (98.13%), and AUC (98.93%) compared with the traditional classifiers. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model has a high performance, which makes it an acceptable and effective solution for automatic sleep disorder diagnosis and feasible in mobile health, wearable devices, access to be used clinically.

title: Ensemble Deep Learning for Brain Tumor Classification Using MRI: A Comparative Study of CNN Architectures
authors: Abdullah Al Mahmud Joy, Md Faizer Islam, Md. Mamun Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: Early and accurate detection of brain tumors is critical for improving patient outcomes; however, it remains challenging due to the variability in tumor morphology. This study presents a comparative evaluation of four deep learning architectures: Custom CNN, MobileNetV2, VGG16, and EfficientNetB0, along with a soft-voting ensemble method for brain tumor classification using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. The data set included balanced images of four classes: glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumors, and no tumors. The preprocessing involved intensity normalization, spatial resizing, and label encoding, with training optimized through class weighting, adaptive learning rate scheduling, and early termination. The interpretability of the model was supported using Grad-CAM visualizations to highlight discriminatory regions. The experimental results show that the proposed ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 98.93%, outperforming EfficientNetB0 (97.62%), MobileNetV2 (97.38%), VGG16 (96.99%), and Custom CNN (96.79%). The ensemble also delivered superior precision, recall, and F1 scores while reducing interclass confusion, particularly between gliomas and meningiomas. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of ensemble learning in combining complementary model strengths and highlight its potential as a scalable, interpretable, and clinically applicable solution for the automated diagnosis of brain tumors.

title: KSiFoldNet: A K-Symplectic Integrated Transformer Network for RNA Structural Prediction with AdamW Optimization
authors: Abir Ehsan Evan, Abir Hassan, Rishan Hasan Tenis, Swandip Singha, Ezaz Ahmed Rajon, Sanjid Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
abstract: The prediction of RNA 3-dimensional (3D) structure given a known sequence is a long standing problem in computational biology that has implications in therapeutics, gene regulation and synthetic biology. Real-world physical constraints in a domain are challenging to inject into common models or neural networks trained using deep learning, and in general, do not generalize well, thus proving of unrealistic predictions due to the lack of physical understanding. In this paper, we present KSiFoldNet - a new K-Symplectic Transformer framework that incorporates the K-Symplectic dynamics to impose structural consistency and uses the AdamW optimiser to obtain better convergence and regularisation. KSiFoldNet produces contextual embeddings for the representation of RNA sequences and bases a second attention-based module in it to capture spatial dependencies. The training goal includes the explicit loss terms informed by symplectic that ensure to get a physically realistic solution. When tested on managed data sources that include atomic coordinates along with annotated residues, KSiFoldNet achieves superior performance on six metrics: MAE, RMSE, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, MAPE, accuracy and TM-score compared with classical regressors (e.g., Ridge, XGBoost), ensemble learners (e.g., LightGBM, GBDT) and contemporary neural defaulting (e.g., CNN, BiLSTM, Transformer variants) on all six metrics. The model attains a state-of-the-art accuracy of 92.1$, a MAE of 0.528, an 0.903 and a TM-score of 0.872, which portrays high structural fidelity and predictive capabilities. The interpretability is achieved by the leading SHAP, LIME, and permutation-based feature importance analysis to determine the key sequence motifs and spatial determinants driving the folding. Ablation tests also show that K-Symplectic constraints and optimization are able to significantly increase model robustness and fidelity. Particularly, the addition of physics-informed priors to Transformer architectures increases RNA folding predictions by more than 50 percent, in structural terms. KSiFoldNet is therefore a holistically competent, interpretable, and biologically impelling machine learning model that can transform how RNA is designed and studied including RNA-based drug discovery and structural genomics.

title: Multi-Area Load Frequency Control in Smart Grids Using Adaptive Sliding Mode Control Techniques
authors: V V Appalanaidu Menda, D. Vijaya Kumar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: The increasing complexity of smart grids and the emergence of renewable generation are clear challenges for load frequency control (LFC) in multiple-area systems with generation and load interconnections. Traditional proportional-integral (PI) and model predictive control approaches often fall short when they fail to acknowledge the uncertainty, interconnectivity, and almost instantaneous time response of modern power systems. In this paper, we investigated the use of an Adaptive Sliding Mode Control (ASMC) design for the Multi-Area Load Frequency Control (MA-LFC) problem. The ASMC design aims to improve robustness of transient performance, minimize frequency excursions, and reduce the inter-area power oscillations when disturbances occur in smart grid operation. ASMC is particularly strong in terms of robustness against uncertainties in the system model and resilience under external disturbances. We describe the integration of the ASMC design with the LFC control architecture in a three-area smart grid which includes thermal, hydro, and renewable generation resources. We solve the LFC control problem involving ASMC and compare that performance against standard PI control and fixed-gain SMC methods including load disturbances and intermittence factors from renewable generation. The simulation results illustrate improved settling time, minimized frequency deviations, and the ability to stabilize inter-area power oscillation in the ASMC design and implementation, which was validated with MATLAB/Simulink. The findings of the research suggest that ASMC is a potential application for achieving highly stable and adaptive LFC in possible smart grids of the future. This ultimately contributes to a more sustainable, secure, and efficient power system that can adapt to changing conditions in real time and provide more reliable electricity over geographically diverse zones.

title: An Intelligent Computer Vision Framework for Enforcing Helmet Compliance and Number Plate Extraction
authors: M Pradeep, G Jyothi Sri Priya, K Vidya Harshini, A Lakshmi Durga, M Jyothi Priyanka
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
abstract: Road safety is a major public concern, with a significant number of fatalities involving two-wheeler riders due to non-compliance with helmet regulations. Traditional enforcement methods rely on physical monitoring is long, inefficient, and difficult to scale. To discourse these tasks, this paper presents an automated computer vision-based system for helmet detection and vehicle number plate recognition. The system employs YOLOv8, a cutting-edge deep learning model for object detection, to accurately identify whether a rider is tiring a helmet. It is designed to work under diverse environmental conditions, ensuring high reliability in real-world applications. When a rider without a helmet is detected, a separate YOLOv8 model is used to locate and extract the vehicle’s number plate, followed by TrOCR, a transformer-based OCR model, to recognize alphanumeric characters for identification. The combination of advanced deep learning techniques enables real-time, efficient, and scalable enforcement of helmet laws. The system's presentation is assessed based on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score and inference speed, representing its effectiveness in practical deployments. By integrating object detection with optical character recognition, this project provides a robust solution for automated traffic monitoring and law enforcement. It not only enhances road safety but also reduces the burden on authorities by offering a seamless, technology-driven approach to ensuring helmet compliance.

title: Fairness in Student Group Formation: Perspectives, Priorities, Compromises, Mechanisms, and Tooling
authors: Matthew Forshaw, Adriana Alexandru, Caitlin Bentley, Vladimiro González-Zelaya, Vangel Ajanovski, Mireilla Bikanga Ada, Julian Brooks, Joshua Burridge, Alex Chao, Rutwa Engineer, Olga Glebova, Tasmina Islam, Mitsuka Kiyohara, Shao-Heng Ko, Elert Smári Kristbergsson, Svetlana Peltsverger, Seán Russell, Maíra Marques Samary, Merel Steenbergen, Carolin Wortmann

title: Adaptive Shared Cascade Navigation Control of Magnetic Microrobots in Unstructured Dynamic Environments_supp2-3658831.mp4
authors: Huaping Wang
abstract: Precise motion control of magnetic microrobots in complex and dynamic environments remains a critical challenge for enabling key applications such as targeted therapy and micromanipulation. Purely manual teleoperation is prone to operator fatigue and error, while fully autonomous systems often lack the robustness and adaptability to handle. Here, we propose a human–machine shared cascade control method for magnetically driven microrobots, which effectively integrates human cognitive intelligence with machine autonomy for collision-free navigation in dynamic environments. The outer-loop hybrid shared control unit smoothly modulates control authority in response to real-time collision risk, dynamically integrating the operator instructions and the autonomous navigation system output guided by the enhanced artificial potential field method to formulate the guidance law. For the inner-loop motion tracking, a data-driven adaptive orientation controller is designed, which integrates a nonlinear feedforward compensator leveraging a Gaussian process regression (GPR) model with a linear feedback controller whose parameters are optimized using the virtual reference feedback tuning (VRFT) method, ensuring fast and precise tracking of the desired motion. The effectiveness of the proposed method was validated through both simulation and physical experiments. In human-subject studies conducted on a physical magnetic actuation platform featuring both static and dynamic obstacle scenarios, quantitative results demonstrate that the shared control strategy significantly outperforms both purely manual and fully autonomous modes across all key metrics, including success rate, task completion time, stability, and safety (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$p \lt 0.001$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Furthermore, successful navigation within a complex gastric model demonstrates the potential of the shared control system for practical application in unstructured environments.

title: A Miniaturized Ingestible Capsule with Integrated ASIC for Energy-Efficient Sensing in the Gastrointestinal Tract
authors: Ramzy Rammouz, Vasileios Adamopoulos, Ivan D. Castro Miller, Wim Sijbers, Ria Sijabat, Dimitrios Firfilionis, Qiuyang Lin, Benjamin Calmé, Tom Torfs
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Circuits and Systems
abstract: Recent efforts have focused on wireless ingestible sensing capsules, but challenges remain in miniaturization, sensor integration, and energy efficiency. This paper presents GISMO-A, an ingestible capsule integrating a custom-designed application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) for low-power biochemical sensing. The ASIC enables pH and oxidation-reduction potential (ORP) measurements at an average power consumption of 172 μW, representing a 70% reduction compared to the previously published GISMO capsule. GISMO-A supports a 6-second measurement interval, resulting in a threefold increase in data density relative to GISMO. Validated through in-vitro and in-vivo experiments, GISMO-A represents a significant advancement in the design of energy-efficient, miniaturized GI Tract sensing systems.

title: Reordering-Resilient Multipath Transport for RDMA-Enabled Cloud Datacenters
authors: Jin Wang, Yang Li, Ruiqian Li, Jalel Ben-Othman, Jinbin Hu
abstract: Remote direct memory access (RDMA) is widely deployed in production data centers to enable low-latency transmission. The current multipath RDMA transmission protocols effectively improve link utilization by allocating traffic to equal-cost parallel paths. To address packet reordering, they struggle to control the level of out-of-order packets by using bitmaps. However, under asymmetric path status and highly dynamic traffic scenarios, a large number of out-of-order packets easily cause bitmap overflow and frequent unnecessary retransmission, resulting in goodput far below throughput. Motivated by this, we present MPTR, an efficient multipath transport with robust reordering for RDMA networks. At its core, MPTR continuously monitors the multipath congestion status at the receiver and distributes the traffic in a congestion-aware manner to proactively reduce the degree of out-of-order and avoid triggering retransmission due to bitmap cache overflow. The NS-3 simulation results show that MPTR effectively reduces unnecessary retransmission and improves goodput under realistic workloads by up to 34%, 49%, and 51% compared to multi-path remote direct memory access (MP-RDMA), ConWeave, and data center quantized congestion notification (DCQCN), respectively.

title: Rotor-Failure-Aware Quadrotors Flight in Unknown Environments
authors: Xiaobin Zhou, Miao Wang, Chengao Li, Can Cui, Ruibin Zhang, Yongchao Wang, Chao Xu, Fei Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Rotor failures in quadrotors may result in high-speed rotation and vibration due to rotor imbalance, which introduces significant challenges for autonomous flight in unknown environments. The main stream approaches against rotor failures rely on fault tolerant control (FTC) and predefined trajectory tracking. To the best of our knowledge, online failure detection and diagnosis (FDD), trajectory planning, and FTC of the post-failure quadrotors in unknown and complex environments have not yet been achieved. This paper presents a rotor-failure-aware quadrotor navigation system designed to mitigate the impacts of rotor imbalance. First, a composite FDD-based nonlinear modelpredictive controller (NMPC), incorporating motor dynamics, is designed to ensure fast failure detection and flight stability. Second, a rotor-failure aware planner is designed to leverage FDD results and spatial-temporal joint optimization, while a LiDAR based quadrotor platform with four anti-torque plates is designed to enable reliable perception under high speed rotation. Lastly, extensive benchmarks against state-of-the-art methods highlight the superior performance of the proposed approach in addressing rotor failures, including propeller unloading and motor stoppage. The experimental results demonstrate, for the first time, that our approach enables autonomous quadrotor flight with rotor failures in challenging en vironments, including cluttered rooms and unknown forests.

title: LMCNet: Lightweight Modality Compensation Network via Knowledge Distillation for Salient Ship Detection under Missing Modality Conditions
authors: Weibao Xue, Jiaqiu Ai, Yanan Zhu, Xinyu Sun, Yong Zhang, Gui Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Salient ship detection is critical for maritime applications that require accurate localization. Although the fusion of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and Automatic Identification System (AIS) data has proven effective in enhancing saliency and suppressing background clutter, practical deployment faces two major limitations: the limited unavailability of AIS data and the high computational overhead of existing multimodal models. These limitations pose a fundamental challenge in balancing detection accuracy and efficiency under missing modality and resource-constrained environments. To address this issue, a Lightweight Modality Compensation Network (LMCNet) is proposed. A multimodal teacher network is trained with SAR and AIS inputs to learn rich and complementary representations. Meanwhile, a compact, single-modality student network that relies only on SAR is designed to support low-cost, real-time deployment. To enable robust knowledge compensation and transfer, this paper designs a knowledge distillation strategy consisting of three modules: structure-aware attention distillation for spatial alignment, cross-head teacher distillation for semantic enhancement, and adaptive loss scheduling for dynamic optimization. This unified design allows the student model to inherit spatial precision and semantic awareness from the teacher, achieving strong performance even with limited input modalities. Extensive experiments on two datasets show that our distilled student model improves Eξ E by 1.39% and Fwβ by 4.37% compared to state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating its superior balance of accuracy and efficiency in real-world deployment scenarios.

title: Automated Synthesis of On-Chip Lumped-Element RF Low-Pass Filters: From Classical to Quasi-Reflectionless and Flat-Group-Delay Designs
authors: Tianqi Chen, Wei Huang, Li Yang, Roberto Gómez-García, Xi Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A novel automated methodology for on-chip RF low-pass filter (LPF) synthesis is presented, minimizing reliance on manual tuning and designer experience. The methodology determines inductor and capacitor values within a predefined filter topology using a unified objective function that incorporates cutoff frequency, insertion loss, reflection behavior, and group-delay (GD) characteristics. Starting from randomly initialized element values in a classical lumped-element LPF, the design space is systematically explored to identify high-quality solutions. The same methodology is extended to synthesize quasi-reflectionless LPFs (QR-LPFs) with absorptive branches and flat-(GD) performance by including reflection and GD terms in the objective function. All LPF designs exhibit stopband transmission zeros (TZs) to achieve sharp-rejection capabilities. Three prototypes were fabricated in a commercial GaAs process to demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach: a reflective LPF with less than 0.4-dB insertion loss at 1 GHz and over 20-dB stopband rejection; a quasi-elliptic QR-LPF maintaining the return loss above 14 dB from DC to 30 GHz; and a QR-LPF achieving a flat-GD variation within ±0.005 ns up to 5.7 GHz.

title: Implicit Neural Representation-Based MRI Reconstruction Method with Sensitivity Map Constraints
authors: Lixuan Rao, Xinlin Zhang, Yiman Huang, Tao Tan, Huihong Sun, Jixiao Sun, Tong Tong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a widely used diagnostic tool, but its long acquisition time limits clinical efficiency. Implicit Neural Representation (INR) has recently shown great potential in scan-specific fast MRI reconstruction without requiring fully sampled training data. However, most existing INR-based methods overlook the structural properties of coil sensitivity maps and apply regularization only to the reconstructed image. To address this, we propose INR-CRISTAL, a joint image and sensitivity estimation framework that introduces explicit regularization on sensitivity maps to leverage their inherent smoothness. Additionally, we design a Frequency-Adaptive SIREN (FA-SIREN) that enables dynamic frequency modulation at each layer, improving the adaptability to heterogeneous spectral components within complex signals. Crucially, our entire framework remains fully self-supervised. Experimental results demonstrate that INR-CRISTAL achieves more accurate sensitivity estimation and superior reconstruction quality, particularly under limited ACS data and high acceleration rates.

title: OBFNet: A Neural Beamforming Network Using Orthonormal Basis Functions
authors: Yanwen Li, Jie Zhang, Huawei Chen, Susanto Rahardja
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Time-domain neural beamformers, such as the filter-and-sum network (FaSNet), can alleviate residual noise and reduce algorithmic latency compared with short-time Fourier transform domain beamformers. However, as a finite impulse response (FIR) beamformer, FaSNet requires a long filter length to achieve high performance. In this work, we therefore propose a time-domain neural beamformer using orthonormal basis functions (OBFs), called OBFNet. We incorporate an OBF module to reduce the filter length while maintaining the filtering gain. Because OBF filters have adjustable poles, OBFNet is more flexible and can be regarded as a generalization of FaSNet. The OBF filter coefficients are optimized in an all-neural form, where a set of learnable parameters is trained for pole selection and a sequential learning network is used to estimate the adaptive weights. We present the impulse response (IR) calculation of OBFNet and perform the standard filter-and-sum operation to recover the target signal. The OBFNet is trained using multi-task learning, where an additional frame-level loss is introduced to constrain the IR. Differences between the IRs of OBFNet and FaSNet can thus be inspected through the loss, which also affects the speech quality. Experimental results show that the proposed OBFNet can outperform existing methods even with a shorter filter length, which is helpful to reduce the latency.

title: Ad-hoc Network for Unmanned Mobile Vehicles: Implementation and Experiments
authors: Jin-Hee Son, Jeong-Min Ma, Hyung-Gon Lee, Nam-Jin Park, Hyo-Sung Ahn
abstract: This study presents an ad-hoc network system for unmanned mobile vehicles (UMVs), with an emphasis on system-level integration and experimental validation using customized hardware and software. A standalone ad-hoc module is developed by integrating open-source firmware and the batman-adv protocol, enabling multi-hop communication while remaining transparent to onboard control software. This design allows UMVs to participate in an ad-hoc network without requiring modifications to existing control or middleware stacks. Outdoor field experiments are conducted to evaluate multi-hop communication performance in terms of throughput, latency, and packet loss under representative experimental conditions. In addition, complementary case studies investigate the functional feasibility of supervisory UMV control and monitoring over multi-hop links. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system provides a practical reference implementation for integrated multi-UMV networking in outdoor experimental settings.
